# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a fuzzy relation. How does it differ from a classical (crisp) relation?**

**Model Answer:**

A fuzzy relation $R$ from $X$ to $Y$ is a fuzzy subset of the Cartesian product $X \times Y$, characterized by a membership function:
$$\mu_R : X \times Y \rightarrow [0, 1]$$

The membership grade $\mu_R(x, y)$ represents the strength of the relation between $x$ and $y$.

**Differences:**

| Aspect | Crisp Relation | Fuzzy Relation |
|--------|----------------|----------------|
| Codomain | $\{0, 1\}$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| Truth value | Absolute | Graduated |
| Example | "$x = y$" | "$x \approx y$" (approximately equal) |
| Mathematical type | Set | Fuzzy set |

Crisp relations are special cases of fuzzy relations where membership grades are restricted to 0 or 1.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State and prove De Morgan's law for the complement of the union of two fuzzy relations.**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** $\overline{R \cup S} = \overline{R} \cap \overline{S}$

**Proof:** For any $(x, y) \in X \times Y$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_{\overline{R \cup S}}(x, y) & = 1 - \mu_{R \cup S}(x, y) \\
& = 1 - \max\{\mu_R(x, y), \mu_S(x, y)\} \\
& = \min\{1 - \mu_R(x, y), 1 - \mu_S(x, y)\} \\
& = \min\{\mu_{\overline{R}}(x, y), \mu_{\overline{S}}(x, y)\} \\
& = \mu_{\overline{R} \cap \overline{S}}(x, y)
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, $\overline{R \cup S} = \overline{R} \cap \overline{S}$. $\blacksquare$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` — **14 Marks**

**(a)** Define fuzzy relation. Explain the operations of union, intersection, and complement on fuzzy relations with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the following two fuzzy relations:

$$R = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.9 & 0.2 \\ 0.1 & 0.6 & 0.8 \end{bmatrix}, \quad S = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7 & 0.3 & 0.4 \\ 0.9 & 0.2 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

Compute: (i) $R \cup S$, (ii) $R \cap S$, (iii) $\overline{R \cap S}$, and (iv) verify De Morgan's law. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Fuzzy Relation:** A fuzzy relation $R$ from $X$ to $Y$ is a fuzzy subset of $X \times Y$, with $\mu_R(x, y) \in [0, 1]$.

**Union ($R \cup S$):** $\mu_{R \cup S}(x, y) = \max\{\mu_R(x, y), \mu_S(x, y)\}$
*Example:* If $\mu_R = 0.6$ and $\mu_S = 0.4$, then $\mu_{R \cup S} = 0.6$.

**Intersection ($R \cap S$):** $\mu_{R \cap S}(x, y) = \min\{\mu_R(x, y), \mu_S(x, y)\}$
*Example:* If $\mu_R = 0.6$ and $\mu_S = 0.4$, then $\mu_{R \cap S} = 0.4$.

**Complement ($\overline{R}$):** $\mu_{\overline{R}}(x, y) = 1 - \mu_R(x, y)$
*Example:* If $\mu_R = 0.6$, then $\mu_{\overline{R}} = 0.4$.

These operations generalize the classical set operations into the fuzzy domain.

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**(i) Compute $R \cup S$ using max:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1,1): \quad & \max\{0.5, 0.7\} = 0.7 \\
(1,2): \quad & \max\{0.9, 0.3\} = 0.9 \\
(1,3): \quad & \max\{0.2, 0.4\} = 0.4 \\
(2,1): \quad & \max\{0.1, 0.9\} = 0.9 \\
(2,2): \quad & \max\{0.6, 0.2\} = 0.6 \\
(2,3): \quad & \max\{0.8, 0.5\} = 0.8
\end{aligned}
$$

$$R \cup S = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7 & 0.9 & 0.4 \\ 0.9 & 0.6 & 0.8 \end{bmatrix}$$

**(ii) Compute $R \cap S$ using min:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1,1): \quad & \min\{0.5, 0.7\} = 0.5 \\
(1,2): \quad & \min\{0.9, 0.3\} = 0.3 \\
(1,3): \quad & \min\{0.2, 0.4\} = 0.2 \\
(2,1): \quad & \min\{0.1, 0.9\} = 0.1 \\
(2,2): \quad & \min\{0.6, 0.2\} = 0.2 \\
(2,3): \quad & \min\{0.8, 0.5\} = 0.5
\end{aligned}
$$

$$R \cap S = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.3 & 0.2 \\ 0.1 & 0.2 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**(iii) Compute $\overline{R \cap S}$ using $1 - x$:**

$$\overline{R \cap S} = \begin{bmatrix} 1-0.5 & 1-0.3 & 1-0.2 \\ 1-0.1 & 1-0.2 & 1-0.5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.7 & 0.8 \\ 0.9 & 0.8 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**(iv) Verify De Morgan's Law: $\overline{R \cup S} = \overline{R} \cap \overline{S}$**

First compute $\overline{R}$:
$$\overline{R} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.1 & 0.8 \\ 0.9 & 0.4 & 0.2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Compute $\overline{S}$:
$$\overline{S} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3 & 0.7 & 0.6 \\ 0.1 & 0.8 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

Compute $\overline{R} \cap \overline{S}$ using min:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1,1): \quad & \min\{0.5, 0.3\} = 0.3 \\
(1,2): \quad & \min\{0.1, 0.7\} = 0.1 \\
(1,3): \quad & \min\{0.8, 0.6\} = 0.6 \\
(2,1): \quad & \min\{0.9, 0.1\} = 0.1 \\
(2,2): \quad & \min\{0.4, 0.8\} = 0.4 \\
(2,3): \quad & \min\{0.2, 0.5\} = 0.2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\overline{R} \cap \overline{S} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3 & 0.1 & 0.6 \\ 0.1 & 0.4 & 0.2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Compute $\overline{R \cup S}$:

$$\overline{R \cup S} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3 & 0.1 & 0.6 \\ 0.1 & 0.4 & 0.2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Both matrices are equal, so De Morgan's law is verified.** ✓

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` — **14 Marks** (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** What is the Cartesian product of two fuzzy sets? Explain with a suitable example. Also discuss its significance in constructing fuzzy relations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given fuzzy sets:

$$A = \left\{\frac{0.3}{x_1}, \frac{0.6}{x_2}, \frac{0.9}{x_3}\right\}, \quad B = \left\{\frac{0.5}{y_1}, \frac{0.8}{y_2}\right\}$$

Compute: (i) $A \times B$, (ii) $A \times \overline{B}$, (iii) $\overline{A} \times B$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Cartesian Product of Fuzzy Sets:** Let $A$ be a fuzzy set on $X$ with membership $\mu_A(x)$ and $B$ be a fuzzy set on $Y$ with membership $\mu_B(y)$. The Cartesian product $A \times B$ is a fuzzy relation on $X \times Y$ defined as:

$$\mu_{A \times B}(x, y) = \min\{\mu_A(x), \mu_B(y)\}$$

**Example:** Let $X = \{x_1, x_2\}$, $Y = \{y_1, y_2\}$ with $A = \{0.4/x_1, 0.7/x_2\}$ and $B = \{0.6/y_1, 0.3/y_2\}$.

$$A \times B = \begin{bmatrix} \min\{0.4, 0.6\} & \min\{0.4, 0.3\} \\ \min\{0.7, 0.6\} & \min\{0.7, 0.3\} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4 & 0.3 \\ 0.6 & 0.3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Significance in Constructing Fuzzy Relations:**

1. It is the **fundamental construction operator** — every fuzzy relation between two independent universes can be viewed as a Cartesian product of fuzzy sets.
2. It enables building **multi-dimensional fuzzy rules** in expert systems (e.g., "IF temperature is HIGH AND pressure is LOW THEN ...").
3. It is the precursor to the **fuzzy relational composition** (covered in Module 3).
4. It provides a **canonical method** to combine fuzzy membership grades across different domains.

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Identify the inputs.**

$A = \{0.3/x_1, 0.6/x_2, 0.9/x_3\}$ (3 elements on $X$)
$B = \{0.5/y_1, 0.8/y_2\}$ (2 elements on $Y$)

Each Cartesian product will be a $3 \times 2$ matrix.

**(i) Compute $A \times B$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(x_1, y_1): \quad & \min\{0.3, 0.5\} = 0.3 \\
(x_1, y_2): \quad & \min\{0.3, 0.8\} = 0.3 \\
(x_2, y_1): \quad & \min\{0.6, 0.5\} = 0.5 \\
(x_2, y_2): \quad & \min\{0.6, 0.8\} = 0.6 \\
(x_3, y_1): \quad & \min\{0.9, 0.5\} = 0.5 \\
(x_3, y_2): \quad & \min\{0.9, 0.8\} = 0.8
\end{aligned}
$$

$$A \times B = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3 & 0.3 \\ 0.5 & 0.6 \\ 0.5 & 0.8 \end{bmatrix}$$

**(ii) Compute $A \times \overline{B}$:**

First, $\overline{B} = \{1 - 0.5/y_1, 1 - 0.8/y_2\} = \{0.5/y_1, 0.2/y_2\}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
(x_1, y_1): \quad & \min\{0.3, 0.5\} = 0.3 \\
(x_1, y_2): \quad & \min\{0.3, 0.2\} = 0.2 \\
(x_2, y_1): \quad & \min\{0.6, 0.5\} = 0.5 \\
(x_2, y_2): \quad & \min\{0.6, 0.2\} = 0.2 \\
(x_3, y_1): \quad & \min\{0.9, 0.5\} = 0.5 \\
(x_3, y_2): \quad & \min\{0.9, 0.2\} = 0.2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$A \times \overline{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3 & 0.2 \\ 0.5 & 0.2 \\ 0.5 & 0.2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**(iii) Compute $\overline{A} \times B$:**

First, $\overline{A} = \{1 - 0.3/x_1, 1 - 0.6/x_2, 1 - 0.9/x_3\} = \{0.7/x_1, 0.4/x_2, 0.1/x_3\}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
(x_1, y_1): \quad & \min\{0.7, 0.5\} = 0.5 \\
(x_1, y_2): \quad & \min\{0.7, 0.8\} = 0.7 \\
(x_2, y_1): \quad & \min\{0.4, 0.5\} = 0.4 \\
(x_2, y_2): \quad & \min\{0.4, 0.8\} = 0.4 \\
(x_3, y_1): \quad & \min\{0.1, 0.5\} = 0.1 \\
(x_3, y_2): \quad & \min\{0.1, 0.8\} = 0.1
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\overline{A} \times B = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.7 \\ 0.4 & 0.4 \\ 0.1 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing max and min** — Students often write min for union and max for intersection. This costs 2-3 marks instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting to apply element-wise** — Each cell must be computed independently; you cannot just add or subtract matrices.
> 3. **Dimension mismatch in Cartesian product** — Ensure $A$ has $m$ elements and $B$ has $n$ elements, and the result is a properly indexed $m \times n$ matrix.
> 4. **Skipping the verification step** in De Morgan's law problems — KTU examiners award bonus marks for explicit verification.
> 5. **Not simplifying the complement** when computing $(A \times \overline{B})$ — first compute $\overline{B}$, then proceed.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick Revision Checklist (Save for Last-Minute Review):**

- **Fuzzy Relation:** A fuzzy subset of $X \times Y$ with $\mu_R : X \times Y \rightarrow [0, 1]$.
- **Union:** $\mu_{R \cup S} = \max\{\mu_R, \mu_S\}$ — represents logical OR.
- **Intersection:** $\mu_{R \cap S} = \min\{\mu_R, \mu_S\}$ — represents logical AND.
- **Complement:** $\mu_{\overline{R}} = 1 - \mu_R$ — represents logical NOT.
- **Cartesian Product:** $\mu_{A \times B}(x, y) = \min\{\mu_A(x), \mu_B(y)\}$ — combines fuzzy sets into a relation.
- **De Morgan's Law 1:** $\overline{R \cup S} = \overline{R} \cap \overline{S}$.
- **De Morgan's Law 2:** $\overline{R \cap S} = \overline{R} \cup \overline{S}$.
- **Involution:** $\overline{\overline{R}} = R$.
- **Union strengthens** the relation (larger values); **intersection weakens** it (smaller values).
- **All values** in a fuzzy relation matrix must lie in $[0, 1]$.
- **Membership matrix** representation is the standard for finite discrete universes.
- **Cartesian product** is the precursor to **fuzzy relational composition** (Module 3).
- The result of a union/intersection of two $m \times n$ relations is again an $m \times n$ relation.
- The Cartesian product of $|X| \times |Y|$ sets yields a relation of size $|X| \times |Y|$.
- For **all derivation questions**, write out cell-by-cell computation; do not skip steps.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick — "U-MAX, I-MIN, C-1-x":**
> - **U**nion = **MAX**
> - **I**ntersection = **MIN**
> - **C**omplement = **1 − x**
> 
> This is the single most important memory anchor for Module 2 — it appears in 80% of KTU problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuzzy Composition - Max-Min, Max-Product
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT523 - FUZZY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fuzzy Composition - Max-Min, Max-Product

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Fuzzy Composition** is the formal mathematical operation of combining two or more fuzzy relations defined on common or overlapping universal sets to produce a new fuzzy relation. For two fuzzy relations $R(A \times B)$ and $S(B \times C)$, the *composition* $S \circ R$ is a fuzzy relation on $A \times C$.

The two principal forms studied at the B.Tech level are:

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Max-Min Composition (Sup-Min Composition):**  
> $$\mu_{S \circ R}(a, c) = \max_{b \in B} \min[\mu_R(a, b),\ \mu_S(b, c)]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Max-Product Composition (Sup-Product Composition):**  
> $$\mu_{S \circ R}(a, c) = \max_{b \in B} [\mu_R(a, b) \cdot \mu_S(b, c)]$$

In matrix form, if $R$ is an $m \times n$ matrix and $S$ is an $n \times p$ matrix, then the resulting relation $S \circ R$ is an $m \times p$ matrix — analogous in *dimension* to standard matrix multiplication, but the arithmetic operators are replaced.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are planning a **trip from City A to City C via City B**, and you have a *fuzzy travel comfort index* for every (A, B) and (B, C) pair.

- The **"min"** operation represents the *weakest link rule* — your comfort on the combined route cannot exceed the worse of the two legs.
- The **"max"** over $b$ represents choosing the *best intermediate city* B that maximizes the combined comfort.
- The **"product"** operation (in Max-Product) represents a *multiplicative penalty model* — if either leg is even slightly weak, the overall rating is strongly scaled down.

> [!NOTE]  
> Think of Max-Min as: *"A chain is only as strong as its weakest link, and we pick the strongest chain."*  
> Think of Max-Product as: *"Each leg scales the other multiplicatively, and we pick the leg that gives the highest scaled result."*

## 1.3 Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Meaning | Range |
|---|---|---|
| $\mu_R(a,b)$ | Membership grade of $(a,b)$ in relation $R$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| $\circ$ | Composition operator | — |
| $\max,\ \min$ | Supremum and Infimum operators over the bridging set $B$ | — |
| $\cdot$ | Standard arithmetic product | $[0, 1]$ |

The standard fuzzy values are bounded in the **unit interval [0, 1]**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of Max-Min Composition (Step-by-Step)

Given:
- $R$ is a fuzzy relation on $A \times B$ of size $m \times n$
- $S$ is a fuzzy relation on $B \times C$ of size $n \times p$

To compute each entry $(i, j)$ of $S \circ R$:

1. **Select the i-th row of $R$** → a vector $[r_{i1}, r_{i2}, \dots, r_{in}]$
2. **Select the j-th column of $S$** → a vector $[s_{1j}, s_{2j}, \dots, s_{nj}]$
3. **Pairwise compute $\min(r_{ik}, s_{kj})$** for $k = 1, 2, \dots, n$
4. **Take $\max_k$ of these minima** — this is the value placed in cell $(i, j)$

> [!NOTE]  
> The "min" comes first inside the max because in fuzzy logic it acts as an **AND (T-norm)** operator, and the outer "max" acts as an **OR (S-norm / S-conorm)**.

## 2.2 Operational Logic of Max-Product Composition

The only change is in step 3:
1. Pairwise compute the **arithmetic product** $r_{ik} \cdot s_{kj}$ for $k = 1, 2, \dots, n$
2. Take the maximum over $k$

The product is a continuous, smooth T-norm (unlike min, which is non-differentiable), so Max-Product is often used in **gradient-based learning** (e.g., fuzzy neural networks).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet

| Composition Type | Mathematical Definition | T-norm Used | S-norm Used | Common Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Max-Min** | $\mu_{S \circ R}(a,c) = \max_b \min[\mu_R(a,b), \mu_S(b,c)]$ | $\min$ | $\max$ | Inference in fuzzy control (Mamdani) |
| **Max-Product** | $\mu_{S \circ R}(a,c) = \max_b [\mu_R(a,b) \cdot \mu_S(b,c)]$ | $\cdot$ (product) | $\max$ | Sugeno-style inference, defuzzification-friendly models |
| **Max-Average** | $\mu_{S \circ R}(a,c) = \max_b \left[\dfrac{\mu_R(a,b) + \mu_S(b,c)}{2}\right]$ | Average | $\max$ | Compromise modelling |
| **Min-Max (Relational)** | $\mu_{R \wedge S} = \min[\mu_R, \mu_S]$ | — | — | NOT a composition (just intersection) |

> [!WARNING]  
> Do not confuse **fuzzy composition** with **fuzzy intersection**. Composition chains relations through an intermediate set; intersection operates point-wise on identically-ordered pairs.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Fuzzy Control (Mamdani)**: Rules of the form *"If Speed is High AND Acceleration is Low, Then Brake Pressure is Medium"* are evaluated using Max-Min composition between the rule-base and the current input vector.
- **Fuzzy Expert Systems (Medical Diagnosis)**: Patient symptom → disease strength → recommended treatment, chained through Max-Min or Max-Product compositions.
- **Pattern Recognition**: Fuzzified feature vectors are matched against prototype relations via Max-Product to identify the most similar class.
- **Decision Support Systems**: Multi-criteria decision matrices are composed to yield an aggregated decision ranking.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Max-Min Composition

### Problem Statement
Let $A = \{a_1, a_2\}$, $B = \{b_1, b_2, b_3\}$, $C = \{c_1, c_2\}$.  
Fuzzy relations:

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7 & 0.5 & 0.2 \\ 0.1 & 0.4 & 0.8 \end{bmatrix}, \quad S = \begin{bmatrix} 0.9 & 0.3 \\ 0.2 & 0.6 \\ 0.5 & 0.7 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Compute $S \circ R$ using **Max-Min composition**.

### Step 1: Determine the Output Dimensions
$R$ is $2 \times 3$, $S$ is $3 \times 2$, so $S \circ R$ will be $2 \times 2$.

### Step 2: Compute Cell $(1, 1)$ of $S \circ R$
Take Row 1 of $R$: $[0.7,\ 0.5,\ 0.2]$ and Column 1 of $S$: $[0.9,\ 0.2,\ 0.5]^T$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
k=1: \min(0.7, 0.9) &= 0.7 \\
k=2: \min(0.5, 0.2) &= 0.2 \\
k=3: \min(0.2, 0.5) &= 0.2
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\mu_{S \circ R}(a_1, c_1) = \max(0.7,\ 0.2,\ 0.2) = 0.7
$$

### Step 3: Compute Cell $(1, 2)$ of $S \circ R$
Row 1 of $R$: $[0.7,\ 0.5,\ 0.2]$; Column 2 of $S$: $[0.3,\ 0.6,\ 0.7]^T$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
k=1: \min(0.7, 0.3) &= 0.3 \\
k=2: \min(0.5, 0.6) &= 0.5 \\
k=3: \min(0.2, 0.7) &= 0.2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\mu_{S \circ R}(a_1, c_2) = \max(0.3,\ 0.5,\ 0.2) = 0.5
$$

### Step 4: Compute Cell $(2, 1)$ of $S \circ R$
Row 2 of $R$: $[0.1,\ 0.4,\ 0.8]$; Column 1 of $S$: $[0.9,\ 0.2,\ 0.5]^T$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
k=1: \min(0.1, 0.9) &= 0.1 \\
k=2: \min(0.4, 0.2) &= 0.2 \\
k=3: \min(0.8, 0.5) &= 0.5
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\mu_{S \circ R}(a_2, c_1) = \max(0.1,\ 0.2,\ 0.5) = 0.5
$$

### Step 5: Compute Cell $(2, 2)$ of $S \circ R$
Row 2 of $R$: $[0.1,\ 0.4,\ 0.8]$; Column 2 of $S$: $[0.3,\ 0.6,\ 0.7]^T$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
k=1: \min(0.1, 0.3) &= 0.1 \\
k=2: \min(0.4, 0.6) &= 0.4 \\
k=3: \min(0.8, 0.7) &= 0.7
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\mu_{S \circ R}(a_2, c_2) = \max(0.1,\ 0.4,\ 0.7) = 0.7
$$

### Final Max-Min Result

$$
S \circ R = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7 & 0.5 \\ 0.5 & 0.7 \end{bmatrix}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Max-Product Composition (Same Inputs)

We re-use the same $R$ and $S$, but apply the product T-norm.

### Cell $(1, 1)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
k=1:\ 0.7 \times 0.9 &= 0.63 \\
k=2:\ 0.5 \times 0.2 &= 0.10 \\
k=3:\ 0.2 \times 0.5 &= 0.10
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\mu_{S \circ R}(a_1, c_1) = \max(0.63,\ 0.10,\ 0.10) = 0.63
$$

### Cell $(1, 2)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
k=1:\ 0.7 \times 0.3 &= 0.21 \\
k=2:\ 0.5 \times 0.6 &= 0.30 \\
k=3:\ 0.2 \times 0.7 &= 0.14
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\mu_{S \circ R}(a_1, c_2) = \max(0.21,\ 0.30,\ 0.14) = 0.30
$$

### Cell $(2, 1)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
k=1:\ 0.1 \times 0.9 &= 0.09 \\
k=2:\ 0.4 \times 0.2 &= 0.08 \\
k=3:\ 0.8 \times 0.5 &= 0.40
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\mu_{S \circ R}(a_2, c_1) = \max(0.09,\ 0.08,\ 0.40) = 0.40
$$

### Cell $(2, 2)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
k=1:\ 0.1 \times 0.3 &= 0.03 \\
k=2:\ 0.4 \times 0.6 &= 0.24 \\
k=3:\ 0.8 \times 0.7 &= 0.56
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\mu_{S \circ R}(a_2, c_2) = \max(0.03,\ 0.24,\ 0.56) = 0.56
$$

### Final Max-Product Result

$$
S \circ R = \begin{bmatrix} 0.63 & 0.30 \\ 0.40 & 0.56 \end{bmatrix}
$$

---

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix (Max-Min vs. Max-Product)

| Property | Max-Min | Max-Product |
|---|---|---|
| Result for $(1,1)$ | $0.7$ | $0.63$ |
| Result for $(1,2)$ | $0.5$ | $0.30$ |
| Result for $(2,1)$ | $0.5$ | $0.40$ |
| Result for $(2,2)$ | $0.7$ | $0.56$ |
| T-norm continuity | Discontinuous (kinks at equal inputs) | Smooth and differentiable |
| Dominant value behaviour | Preserves the maximum cell value from either operand | Always strictly less than or equal to the max-min value |
| Output range | Same as inputs: $[0, 1]$ | Same as inputs: $[0, 1]$ |
| Use in fuzzy inference | **Mamdani** (most common) | **Sugeno / Larsen** product inference |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Observation Rule:** For the same matrices, $\mu_{\text{Max-Product}}(a, c) \le \mu_{\text{Max-Min}}(a, c)$ because $x \cdot y \le \min(x, y)$ for $x, y \in [0, 1]$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of Fuzzy Composition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Fuzzy Relation R on A times B"]:::input
    B["Fuzzy Relation S on B times C"]:::input
    P1["Select Row i of R"]:::proc
    P2["Select Column j of S"]:::proc
    P3["Pairwise T-norm Operation"]:::proc
    P4["S-norm Aggregation over Bridging Set B"]:::proc
    P5["Output Entry mu of S circ R at i and j"]:::out
    Q1["MIN operator for Max-Min"]:::t1
    Q2["PRODUCT operator for Max-Product"]:::t2
    R1["MAX operator aggregates intermediate results"]:::t3

    A --> P1
    B --> P2
    P1 --> P3
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5
    P3 -.chooses.-> Q1
    P3 -.chooses.-> Q2
    P4 -.always.-> R1

    classDef input fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1,color:#0c4a6e
    classDef proc fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#78350f
    classDef t1 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#14532d
    classDef t2 fill:#fae8ff,stroke:#7e22ce,color:#581c87
    classDef t3 fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#7f1d1d
    classDef out fill:#f1f5f9,stroke:#334155,color:#0f172a
```

## 4.2 Data-Flow Schematic for Matrix Composition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph M1["Relation R: m rows by n cols"]
        R1["Row 1: r11 r12 ... r1n"]
        R2["Row 2: r21 r22 ... r2n"]
        Ri["Row i: ri1 ri2 ... rin"]
    end
    subgraph M2["Relation S: n rows by p cols"]
        S1["Col 1: s11 / s21 / ... / sn1"]
        S2["Col 2: s12 / s22 / ... / sn2"]
        Sj["Col j: s1j / s2j / ... / snj"]
    end
    subgraph OUT["Output S circ R: m rows by p cols"]
        Oij["Cell i,j: max over k of T norm of rik and skj"]
    end
    Ri -- bridging set B --> Oij
    Sj -- bridging set B --> Oij

    classDef mat fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#1e3a8a
    classDef cell fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,color:#14532d
    class M1 mat
    class M2 mat
    class OUT cell
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow: Choosing the Right Composition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Need to compose two fuzzy relations"]:::start
    Q1{"Application area?"}:::dnode
    A1["Fuzzy Control / Mamdani Inference"]:::ctrl
    A2["Pattern Recognition / Sugeno Inference"]:::ctrl
    A3["Theoretical Proofs / Lattice Operations"]:::ctrl
    E1["Use Max-Min Composition"]:::rec
    E2["Use Max-Product Composition"]:::rec
    E3["Use Max-Min (most general property holder)"]:::rec

    Start --> Q1
    Q1 -- "Industrial controllers" --> A1 --> E1
    Q1 -- "Neural fuzzy / classification" --> A2 --> E2
    Q1 -- "Lattice and algebraic properties" --> A3 --> E3
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]`
**Define Max-Min composition of two fuzzy relations. State the condition for its existence.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Let $R$ be a fuzzy relation on $A \times B$ and $S$ be a fuzzy relation on $B \times C$. The Max-Min composition $S \circ R$ is a fuzzy relation on $A \times C$ defined as:
$$\mu_{S \circ R}(a, c) = \max_{b \in B} \min[\mu_R(a, b),\ \mu_S(b, c)]$$
- **Existence condition (1 mark):** The set $B$ must be the common universe of the second dimension of $R$ and the first dimension of $S$ (i.e., the relations must be *conformable* for composition — second index set of $R$ must match the first index set of $S$).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Part A Pool]`
**Differentiate between Max-Min and Max-Product composition. Give the formula for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Max-Min | Max-Product |
|---|---|---|
| T-norm | $\min$ | $\cdot$ (arithmetic product) |
| Formula | $\max_b \min[\mu_R(a,b), \mu_S(b,c)]$ | $\max_b [\mu_R(a,b) \cdot \mu_S(b,c)]$ |
| Continuity | Discontinuous at equal inputs | Smooth and differentiable |
| Inference style | Mamdani | Sugeno / Larsen |

For the same relations, the Max-Product value is always **less than or equal to** the Max-Min value at every cell.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Consider the fuzzy relations:

$$R = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.3 \\ 0.2 & 0.9 \end{bmatrix} \text{ on } A \times B, \quad S = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.7 & 0.1 \\ 0.4 & 0.6 & 0.8 \end{bmatrix} \text{ on } B \times C$$

Compute $S \circ R$ using **Max-Min composition**. Show every step.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using the same $R$ and $S$, compute $S \circ R$ using **Max-Product composition** and compare the two resulting matrices element-wise.

#### Model Solution to (a):

$R$ is $2 \times 2$, $S$ is $2 \times 3$ → Output is $2 \times 3$.

**Cell $(1,1)$:** $\max[\min(0.6, 0.5), \min(0.3, 0.4)] = \max[0.5, 0.3] = 0.5$

**Cell $(1,2)$:** $\max[\min(0.6, 0.7), \min(0.3, 0.6)] = \max[0.6, 0.3] = 0.6$

**Cell $(1,3)$:** $\max[\min(0.6, 0.1), \min(0.3, 0.8)] = \max[0.1, 0.3] = 0.3$

**Cell $(2,1)$:** $\max[\min(0.2, 0.5), \min(0.9, 0.4)] = \max[0.2, 0.4] = 0.4$

**Cell $(2,2)$:** $\max[\min(0.2, 0.7), \min(0.9, 0.6)] = \max[0.2, 0.6] = 0.6$

**Cell $(2,3)$:** $\max[\min(0.2, 0.1), \min(0.9, 0.8)] = \max[0.1, 0.8] = 0.8$

$$
S \circ R = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.6 & 0.3 \\ 0.4 & 0.6 & 0.8 \end{bmatrix}
$$

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Cell $(1,1)$:** $\max(0.6 \times 0.5,\ 0.3 \times 0.4) = \max(0.30,\ 0.12) = 0.30$

**Cell $(1,2)$:** $\max(0.6 \times 0.7,\ 0.3 \times 0.6) = \max(0.42,\ 0.18) = 0.42$

**Cell $(1,3)$:** $\max(0.6 \times 0.1,\ 0.3 \times 0.8) = \max(0.06,\ 0.24) = 0.24$

**Cell $(2,1)$:** $\max(0.2 \times 0.5,\ 0.9 \times 0.4) = \max(0.10,\ 0.36) = 0.36$

**Cell $(2,2)$:** $\max(0.2 \times 0.7,\ 0.9 \times 0.6) = \max(0.14,\ 0.54) = 0.54$

**Cell $(2,3)$:** $\max(0.2 \times 0.1,\ 0.9 \times 0.8) = \max(0.02,\ 0.72) = 0.72$

$$
S \circ R \big|_{\text{Max-Prod}} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.30 & 0.42 & 0.24 \\ 0.36 & 0.54 & 0.72 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Comparison (1 mark for the qualitative comparison):** Every entry in the Max-Product matrix is strictly less than its Max-Min counterpart (e.g., $0.30 < 0.5$, $0.54 < 0.6$, $0.72 < 0.8$), confirming the theorem $\mu_{\text{prod}} \le \mu_{\text{min}}$ for $x, y \in [0, 1]$.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Alternative Choice]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define a *fuzzy relation*. Given:
$$R = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4 & 0.9 & 0.2 \\ 0.7 & 0.5 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad S = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8 & 0.3 \\ 0.6 & 0.4 \\ 0.2 & 0.9 \end{bmatrix}$$
Find $S \circ R$ using **Max-Min composition**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the same matrices, compute $S \circ R$ using **Max-Product composition**. State two applications of fuzzy composition in real-world fuzzy systems.

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Fuzzy Relation Definition (2 marks):** A fuzzy relation $R$ on $A \times B$ is a fuzzy set on the Cartesian product $A \times B$, characterized by a membership function $\mu_R: A \times B \to [0, 1]$, where $\mu_R(a, b)$ represents the strength of the relationship between $a$ and $b$.

**Cell $(1,1)$:** $\max[\min(0.4, 0.8), \min(0.9, 0.6), \min(0.2, 0.2)] = \max[0.4, 0.6, 0.2] = 0.6$

**Cell $(1,2)$:** $\max[\min(0.4, 0.3), \min(0.9, 0.4), \min(0.2, 0.9)] = \max[0.3, 0.4, 0.2] = 0.4$

**Cell $(2,1)$:** $\max[\min(0.7, 0.8), \min(0.5, 0.6), \min(0.1, 0.2)] = \max[0.7, 0.5, 0.1] = 0.7$

**Cell $(2,2)$:** $\max[\min(0.7, 0.3), \min(0.5, 0.4), \min(0.1, 0.9)] = \max[0.3, 0.4, 0.1] = 0.4$

$$
S \circ R = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.4 \\ 0.7 & 0.4 \end{bmatrix}
$$

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Cell $(1,1)$:** $\max(0.4 \times 0.8,\ 0.9 \times 0.6,\ 0.2 \times 0.2) = \max(0.32,\ 0.54,\ 0.04) = 0.54$

**Cell $(1,2)$:** $\max(0.4 \times 0.3,\ 0.9 \times 0.4,\ 0.2 \times 0.9) = \max(0.12,\ 0.36,\ 0.18) = 0.36$

**Cell $(2,1)$:** $\max(0.7 \times 0.8,\ 0.5 \times 0.6,\ 0.1 \times 0.2) = \max(0.56,\ 0.30,\ 0.02) = 0.56$

**Cell $(2,2)$:** $\max(0.7 \times 0.3,\ 0.5 \times 0.4,\ 0.1 \times 0.9) = \max(0.21,\ 0.20,\ 0.09) = 0.21$

$$
S \circ R \big|_{\text{Max-Prod}} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.54 & 0.36 \\ 0.56 & 0.21 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Two Applications of Fuzzy Composition (2 marks):**
1. **Fuzzy Inference Engines (Mamdani Controller):** Composing the fuzzified input vector with the fuzzy rule-base to produce the aggregated output fuzzy set.
2. **Medical Diagnosis Expert Systems:** Chaining *symptom → disease strength* with *disease → treatment* relations to recommend the most suitable therapy.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common places where students lose marks in Max-Min / Max-Product questions:**
> 1. **Row-Column Index Mismatch (–2 marks):** Computing $R \circ S$ when the problem asks for $S \circ R$ (or vice versa). Always verify that the *second* index set of the first relation matches the *first* index set of the second relation.
> 2. **Confusing $\min$ and $\max$ order (–1 mark):** The pairing is always $\min$ *first* (over $b$), then $\max$ *over* $b$ — never the reverse.
> 3. **Using product where min is required (–3 marks):** A single wrong T-norm operator invalidates the entire matrix.
> 4. **Forgetting to show step-by-step pairing (–2 marks):** Examiners expect explicit $\min$ and $\max$ evaluations for every cell, not just the final matrix.
> 5. **Off-by-one indexing errors (–1 mark):** Use $b_1, b_2, \dots, b_n$ consistently and label every intermediate value.
> 6. **Writing $\min[\mu_R, \mu_S]$ as intersection $R \cap S$ (–1 mark):** Composition is **not** intersection; the dimension must reduce.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Fuzzy Composition** combines $R(A \times B)$ and $S(B \times C)$ to produce a relation on $A \times C$.
- **Max-Min Formula:** $\mu_{S \circ R}(a, c) = \max_{b \in B} \min[\mu_R(a, b), \mu_S(b, c)]$
- **Max-Product Formula:** $\mu_{S \circ R}(a, c) = \max_{b \in B} [\mu_R(a, b) \cdot \mu_S(b, c)]$
- **Conformability:** $R$ is $m \times n$, $S$ is $n \times p$ → $S \circ R$ is $m \times p$. The inner dimension $n$ must match.
- **Algorithmic Steps:** Pick row $i$ of $R$, pick column $j$ of $S$, apply T-norm pairwise, aggregate with $\max$ over the bridging set.
- **Inequality Property:** For $x, y \in [0, 1]$, $x \cdot y \le \min(x, y)$, hence Max-Product $\le$ Max-Min entry-wise.
- **Mamdani vs. Sugeno:** Max-Min pairs with Mamdani inference; Max-Product pairs with Sugeno / Larsen product inference.
- **Differentiability:** Max-Min is non-smooth; Max-Product is smooth — relevant for gradient-based learning.
- **Composition ≠ Intersection:** Composition chains relations through an intermediate set; intersection is point-wise on identically ordered pairs.
- **Output Range:** Both compositions preserve membership values in the unit interval $[0, 1]$.
- **Step-by-step writing** is mandatory in KTU 14-mark questions — never skip intermediate $\min$ or product calculations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Extension Principle - Fuzzy Arithmetic, Fuzzy Numbers, Arithmetic Operations on Fuzzy Numbers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT523 - FUZZY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Extension Principle - Fuzzy Arithmetic, Fuzzy Numbers, Arithmetic Operations on Fuzzy Numbers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Zadeh's Extension Principle — The Foundation of Fuzzy Arithmetic

**Definition (Zadeh's Extension Principle):**
Let $f: X \rightarrow Y$ be a crisp (classical) mapping from universe $X$ to universe $Y$. Let $\tilde{A}$ be a fuzzy set in $X$ with membership function $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$. Then the Extension Principle defines the image of $\tilde{A}$ under $f$, denoted $f(\tilde{A})$ or $\tilde{B}$, as a fuzzy set in $Y$ with the membership function:

$$\mu_{\tilde{B}}(y) = \begin{cases} \sup\limits_{x \in f^{-1}(y)} \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x), & \text{if } f^{-1}(y) \neq \emptyset \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

In words: **the membership grade of $y$ in the image fuzzy set is the highest membership grade of any $x$ that maps to $y$.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multi-Variable Extension Principle (Critical for KTU):**
> For $f: X_1 \times X_2 \times \cdots \times X_n \rightarrow Y$ and fuzzy sets $\tilde{A}_1, \tilde{A}_2, \ldots, \tilde{A}_n$:
>
> $$\mu_{\tilde{B}}(y) = \sup_{\substack{(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n) \in X_1 \times \cdots \times X_n \\ f(x_1, \ldots, x_n) = y}} \min\left\{\mu_{\tilde{A}_1}(x_1), \mu_{\tilde{A}_2}(x_2), \ldots, \mu_{\tilde{A}_n}(x_n)\right\}$$
>
> The `min` operator is the **fuzzy intersection (t-norm)**, and `sup` is the **fuzzy union (s-norm)** — this is exactly the crisp-to-fuzzy bridge your examiner expects.

### 1.2 Intuition — The "Blurry Calculator" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Fog of Numbers":**
> Imagine a regular calculator that accepts only crisp numbers like `5`. Now suppose you input a *vague* number — say, "about 5" — meaning it could be 4, 5, 6, or anywhere nearby, each with a certain **degree of confidence**. The Extension Principle is the rule that tells the calculator: *"Take the maximum confidence among all crisp inputs that could produce the same crisp output."* It is the **mathematical rulebook for computing when the inputs are uncertain.**

The Extension Principle is the bridge that lifts every classical operation (addition, multiplication, etc.) from the realm of exact numbers to the realm of **fuzzy numbers** — without losing the meaning of the operation.

## 1.3 Fuzzy Numbers — Formal Definition

**Definition (Fuzzy Number):**
A fuzzy set $\tilde{A}$ defined on the real line $\mathbb{R}$ is called a **fuzzy number** if it satisfies the following three axioms:

1. **Normality:** $\exists \, x_0 \in \mathbb{R}$ such that $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x_0) = 1$ (the peak reaches full membership).
2. **Convexity:** $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda)x_2) \geq \min(\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x_1), \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x_2))$ for all $\lambda \in [0,1]$ and all $x_1, x_2 \in \mathbb{R}$.
3. **Bounded Support:** The support $(\tilde{A})^0 = \{x \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) > 0\}$ is a bounded subset of $\mathbb{R}$.
4. **Piecewise Continuity:** $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$ is upper semi-continuous.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equivalently:** A fuzzy number is a fuzzy set whose every $\alpha$-cut, for $\alpha \in (0, 1]$, is a **closed, bounded interval** of the form $[a_L(\alpha), a_R(\alpha)]$.
>
> This $\alpha$-cut characterization is the **workhorse of fuzzy arithmetic** in KTU problems.

## 1.4 Common Families of Fuzzy Numbers

| Type | Notation | Membership Function |
|------|----------|---------------------|
| **Triangular Fuzzy Number (TFN)** | $\tilde{A} = (a, b, c)$ | Peak at $b$, linearly rising from $a$ and falling to $c$ |
| **Trapezoidal Fuzzy Number (TrFN)** | $\tilde{A} = (a, b, c, d)$ | Flat plateau from $b$ to $c$ at membership 1 |
| **Gaussian Fuzzy Number** | $\tilde{A} = (m, \sigma)$ | $\mu(x) = \exp(-(x-m)^2 / 2\sigma^2)$ |
| **Crisp Number** (boundary case) | $\tilde{A} = (k, k, k)$ | $\mu(k) = 1$, else 0 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The $\alpha$-Cut Approach — Why It Works

The $\alpha$-cut of a fuzzy set $\tilde{A}$ is defined as the crisp set:

$$A_\alpha = \{x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \geq \alpha\}, \quad \alpha \in (0, 1]$$

**Key Theorem (Decomposition Theorem):** Every fuzzy set can be reconstructed from its $\alpha$-cuts:

$$\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = \sup_{\alpha \in (0, 1]} \min(\alpha, \chi_{A_\alpha}(x))$$

This is why performing operations on **intervals** (the $\alpha$-cuts) and assembling them back automatically gives us operations on fuzzy numbers. The process is:

1. Decompose each fuzzy number into its family of $\alpha$-cut intervals.
2. Perform the arithmetic on these **intervals** using interval arithmetic.
3. Reconstruct the result using the union of all $\alpha$-cut intervals.

## 2.2 Interval Arithmetic Foundation

For two closed intervals $A = [a_1, a_2]$ and $B = [b_1, b_2]$:

| Operation | Result | Notes |
|-----------|--------|-------|
| Addition | $[a_1 + b_1, \ a_2 + b_2]$ | Always valid |
| Subtraction | $[a_1 - b_2, \ a_2 - b_1]$ | Order of bounds **flips** |
| Multiplication (positive intervals) | $[\min(a_1 b_1, a_1 b_2, a_2 b_1, a_2 b_2), \ \max(\cdots)]$ | Use all 4 products |
| Division | $[a_1, a_2] \cdot [1/b_2, 1/b_1]$ | Requires $0 \notin B$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why four products for multiplication?**
> Without knowing the signs of $a_1, a_2, b_1, b_2$, the maximum and minimum could come from any of the four combinations. For *positive* fuzzy numbers (the common KTU case), this simplifies to $[a_1 b_1, a_2 b_2]$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Fuzzy Arithmetic Master Table

For fuzzy numbers $\tilde{A}, \tilde{B}$ with $\alpha$-cuts $A_\alpha = [a_L(\alpha), a_R(\alpha)]$ and $B_\alpha = [b_L(\alpha), b_R(\alpha)]$:

| # | Operation | Resulting $\alpha$-cut $(\tilde{A} \odot \tilde{B})_\alpha$ | Conditions |
|---|-----------|------------------------------------------------------------|------------|
| 1 | **Addition** | $[a_L(\alpha) + b_L(\alpha), \ a_R(\alpha) + b_R(\alpha)]$ | Always |
| 2 | **Subtraction** | $[a_L(\alpha) - b_R(\alpha), \ a_R(\alpha) - b_L(\alpha)]$ | Always |
| 3 | **Multiplication** | $[\min(a_L b_L, a_L b_R, a_R b_L, a_R b_R), \ \max(\cdots)]$ | General case |
| 4 | **Multiplication (positive)** | $[a_L(\alpha) \cdot b_L(\alpha), \ a_R(\alpha) \cdot b_R(\alpha)]$ | $a_L, b_L \geq 0$ |
| 5 | **Division** | $[a_L(\alpha), a_R(\alpha)] \cdot [1/b_R(\alpha), 1/b_L(\alpha)]$ | $0 \notin B_\alpha$ |
| 6 | **Scalar multiple** ($\lambda \tilde{A}$), $\lambda \geq 0$ | $[\lambda a_L(\alpha), \lambda a_R(\alpha)]$ | — |
| 7 | **Scalar multiple** $\lambda < 0$ | $[\lambda a_R(\alpha), \lambda a_L(\alpha)]$ | Bounds swap |

### 2.4 Closed-Form Endpoints for Triangular Fuzzy Numbers

For $\tilde{A} = (a, b, c)$ and $\tilde{B} = (d, e, f)$, the $\alpha$-cuts are:

$$A_\alpha = [a + \alpha(b-a), \ c - \alpha(c-b)]$$
$$B_\alpha = [d + \alpha(e-d), \ f - \alpha(f-e)]$$

Therefore the results are also triangular:

$$\tilde{A} + \tilde{B} = (a+d, \ b+e, \ c+f)$$
$$\tilde{A} - \tilde{B} = (a-f, \ b-e, \ c-d)$$

### 2.5 Closed-Form Endpoints for Trapezoidal Fuzzy Numbers

For $\tilde{A} = (a, b, c, d)$ and $\tilde{B} = (p, q, r, s)$:

$$\tilde{A} + \tilde{B} = (a+p, \ b+q, \ c+r, \ d+s)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Fuzzy arithmetic is used in **fuzzy control systems, risk analysis under uncertainty, civil engineering (load + factor of safety), inventory models with vague demand, and financial forecasting where inputs are linguistic estimates like "around 10% growth."**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Triangular Fuzzy Number from its Definition

**Given:** $\tilde{A} = (a, b, c)$ with $a \leq b \leq c$.

The membership function is:

$$\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x \leq a \\ \dfrac{x - a}{b - a}, & a \leq x \leq b \\ \dfrac{c - x}{c - b}, & b \leq x \leq c \\ 0, & x \geq c \end{cases}$$

**Derivation of the left endpoint of the $\alpha$-cut:**
For $x \in [a, b]$, we have $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = \dfrac{x - a}{b - a} \geq \alpha$, which gives:

$$x \geq a + \alpha(b - a)$$

**Derivation of the right endpoint of the $\alpha$-cut:**
For $x \in [b, c]$, we have $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = \dfrac{c - x}{c - b} \geq \alpha$, which gives:

$$x \leq c - \alpha(c - b)$$

Hence:

$$A_\alpha = [a + \alpha(b - a), \ c - \alpha(c - b)]$$

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Addition of Two TFNs

**Problem:** Compute $\tilde{A} + \tilde{B}$ where $\tilde{A} = (1, 2, 4)$ and $\tilde{B} = (3, 5, 8)$.

**Step 1 — Write the $\alpha$-cuts:**

$$A_\alpha = [1 + \alpha(1), \ 4 - \alpha(2)] = [1 + \alpha, \ 4 - 2\alpha]$$
$$B_\alpha = [3 + \alpha(2), \ 8 - \alpha(3)] = [3 + 2\alpha, \ 8 - 3\alpha]$$

**Step 2 — Apply the addition formula (positive intervals):**

$$(A + B)_\alpha = [a_L + b_L, \ a_R + b_R]$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$(A + B)_\alpha = [(1 + \alpha) + (3 + 2\alpha), \ (4 - 2\alpha) + (8 - 3\alpha)]$$

$$(A + B)_\alpha = [4 + 3\alpha, \ 12 - 5\alpha]$$

**Step 4 — Identify the result as a TFN.** Comparing with $A_\alpha = [a + \alpha(b-a), c - \alpha(c-b)]$:

- Left endpoint: $4 + 3\alpha$ → $a = 4$, slope $3$ → $b - a = 3$ → $b = 7$
- Right endpoint: $12 - 5\alpha$ → $c = 12$, slope $5$ → $c - b = 5$ → $b = 7$ ✓

$$\boxed{\tilde{A} + \tilde{B} = (4, \ 7, \ 12)}$$

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Subtraction of Two TFNs

**Problem:** Compute $\tilde{A} - \tilde{B}$ for the same $\tilde{A} = (1,2,4)$ and $\tilde{B} = (3,5,8)$.

**Step 1 — Apply the subtraction formula on the $\alpha$-cuts:**

$$(A - B)_\alpha = [a_L - b_R, \ a_R - b_L]$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$(A - B)_\alpha = [(1 + \alpha) - (8 - 3\alpha), \ (4 - 2\alpha) - (3 + 2\alpha)]$$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**

$$(A - B)_\alpha = [1 + \alpha - 8 + 3\alpha, \ 4 - 2\alpha - 3 - 2\alpha]$$
$$(A - B)_\alpha = [-7 + 4\alpha, \ 1 - 4\alpha]$$

**Step 4 — Identify the result as a TFN:**

- Left endpoint: $-7 + 4\alpha$ → leftmost $a = -7$, slope $4$ → $b = -7 + 4 = -3$
- Right endpoint: $1 - 4\alpha$ → rightmost $c = 1$, slope $-4$ → $b = 1 - 4 = -3$ ✓

$$\boxed{\tilde{A} - \tilde{B} = (-7, \ -3, \ 1)}$$

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Multiplication of Two Positive TFNs

**Problem:** Compute $\tilde{A} \times \tilde{B}$ for $\tilde{A} = (1, 2, 3)$ and $\tilde{B} = (2, 3, 4)$ (both positive).

**Step 1 — $\alpha$-cuts:**

$$A_\alpha = [1 + \alpha, \ 3 - \alpha]$$
$$B_\alpha = [2 + \alpha, \ 4 - \alpha]$$

**Step 2 — Use the simplified positive-interval multiplication formula:**

$$(A \cdot B)_\alpha = [a_L \cdot b_L, \ a_R \cdot b_R]$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$(A \cdot B)_\alpha = [(1+\alpha)(2+\alpha), \ (3-\alpha)(4-\alpha)]$$

**Step 4 — Expand the products:**

$$(A \cdot B)_\alpha = [2 + 3\alpha + \alpha^2, \ 12 - 7\alpha + \alpha^2]$$

> [!NOTE]
> The presence of $\alpha^2$ shows the result is **NOT a triangular fuzzy number** — it is a quadratic (parabolic) fuzzy number. This is a common exam trap! The product of two TFNs is generally **not triangular**, only **piecewise quadratic**.

**Step 5 — Reconstruct membership function (for $\alpha \in [0,1]$):**

For each output value $y$, the membership is the $\alpha$ at which $y$ enters the result. We solve:

- Left boundary $y = 2 + 3\alpha + \alpha^2$ → $\alpha^2 + 3\alpha + (2 - y) = 0$ → $\alpha = \dfrac{-3 + \sqrt{1 + 4y}}{2}$ for $y \in [2, 6]$.
- Right boundary $y = 12 - 7\alpha + \alpha^2$ → $\alpha^2 - 7\alpha + (12 - y) = 0$ → $\alpha = \dfrac{7 - \sqrt{1 + 4y}}{2}$ for $y \in [6, 12]$.

So the membership function is parabolic, peaking at $y = 6$ with $\mu = 1$ (when $\alpha = 1$, product of peaks $2 \times 3 = 6$).

## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — Division of Two Positive TFNs

**Problem:** Compute $\tilde{A} / \tilde{B}$ for $\tilde{A} = (2, 4, 6)$ and $\tilde{B} = (1, 2, 4)$.

**Step 1 — $\alpha$-cuts:**

$$A_\alpha = [2 + 2\alpha, \ 6 - 2\alpha]$$
$$B_\alpha = [1 + \alpha, \ 4 - \alpha]$$

**Step 2 — Apply the division formula (positive, $0 \notin B_\alpha$):**

$$(A / B)_\alpha = \left[ \frac{a_L}{b_R}, \ \frac{a_R}{b_L} \right] = \left[ \frac{2 + 2\alpha}{4 - \alpha}, \ \frac{6 - 2\alpha}{1 + \alpha} \right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **General rule for division of positive intervals $[p, q]/[r, s]$:** $\left[\dfrac{p}{s}, \dfrac{q}{r}\right]$ — note the **cross-multiplication** of bounds.

**Step 3 — Find the result's range:**
- Minimum: at $\alpha = 0$: $[2/4, 6/1] = [0.5, 6]$
- Maximum: at $\alpha = 1$: $[4/3, 4/2] = [1.333, 2]$
- Peak (most credible value): at $\alpha = 1$, the interval $[1.333, 2]$ — the modal output is $4/2 = 2$.

The result is again a **non-triangular** fuzzy number with a curved (hyperbolic) shape.

## 3.6 Proof: The Result of Adding Two TFNs is a TFN

**Claim:** If $\tilde{A} = (a, b, c)$ and $\tilde{B} = (d, e, f)$, then $\tilde{A} + \tilde{B} = (a+d, b+e, c+f)$.

**Proof:**
By the addition formula on $\alpha$-cuts:

$$(A + B)_\alpha = [a + \alpha(b-a) + d + \alpha(e-d), \ c - \alpha(c-b) + f - \alpha(f-e)]$$

$$(A + B)_\alpha = [(a+d) + \alpha((b-a) + (e-d)), \ (c+f) - \alpha((c-b) + (f-e))]$$

For this to be a TFN of the form $(L, M, R)$, the slope of the left rising portion must equal the **horizontal distance** from $L$ to $M$, and similarly the slope of the right falling portion must equal $R - M$:

- Left: $M - L = (b+e) - (a+d) = (b-a) + (e-d)$ ✓
- Right: $R - M = (c+f) - (b+e) = (c-b) + (f-e)$ ✓

Therefore $\tilde{A} + \tilde{B} = (a+d, b+e, c+f)$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.7 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Operation on TFN $\tilde{A} = (a, b, c)$, $\tilde{B} = (d, e, f)$ | Result Type | Closed Form |
|---------------------------------------------------------------|-------------|-------------|
| $\tilde{A} + \tilde{B}$ | TFN | $(a+d, b+e, c+f)$ |
| $\tilde{A} - \tilde{B}$ | TFN | $(a-f, b-e, c-d)$ |
| $\tilde{A} \times \tilde{B}$ (positive) | Quadratic fuzzy | Parabolic, NOT TFN |
| $\tilde{A} \div \tilde{B}$ (positive, $d > 0$) | Hyperbolic fuzzy | Rational, NOT TFN |
| $k \cdot \tilde{A}$, $k > 0$ | TFN | $(ka, kb, kc)$ |
| $-\tilde{A}$ | TFN | $(-c, -b, -a)$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Map: How Fuzzy Arithmetic Works (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Crisp Function f: X to Y"] --> B["Identify Domain Fuzzy Inputs"]
    B["Identify Domain Fuzzy Inputs"] --> C["Compute Alpha Cuts for Each Input"]
    C["Compute Alpha Cuts for Each Input"] --> D["Apply Crisp Function to Intervals"]
    D["Apply Crisp Function to Intervals"] --> E["Collect Resulting Alpha Cut Intervals"]
    E["Collect Resulting Alpha Cut Intervals"] --> F["Apply Decomposition Theorem"]
    F["Apply Decomposition Theorem"] --> G["Output Fuzzy Set in Y"]
    G["Output Fuzzy Set in Y"] --> H["Membership Function mu of Result"]

    style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000

```

## 4.2 Hierarchical Classification of Fuzzy Numbers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F0["Fuzzy Set on Real Line"] --> N1{"Normal at x0 with mu equals 1"}
    F0 --> C1{"Convex Membership"}
    F0 --> B1{"Bounded Support"}

    C1 --> FN
    B1 --> FN
    FN --> TFN["Triangular Fuzzy Number TFN form a b c"]
    FN --> TRFN["Trapezoidal Fuzzy Number TrFN form a b c d"]
    FN --> GFN["Gaussian Fuzzy Number form m sigma"]
    FN --> IFN["Interval Valued Fuzzy Number"]
    FN --> CFN["Crisp Number form k k k as special case"]
    TFN --> LR["Piecewise Linear Membership"]
    TRFN --> LR
    GFN --> SM["Smooth Bell Shaped Membership"]
    IFN --> FG["Flat Top Fuzzy Number"]

    style FN fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000

```

## 4.3 Process Flow: $\alpha$-Cut Interval Arithmetic Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Input Fuzzy Number A with TFN a b c"] --> P1["Parse Three Parameters"]
    S2["Input Fuzzy Number B with TFN d e f"] --> P2["Parse Three Parameters"]
    P1 --> AC1["Compute A alpha equals a plus alpha times b minus a, c minus alpha times c minus b"]
    P2 --> AC2["Compute B alpha equals d plus alpha times e minus d, f minus alpha times f minus e"]
    AC1 --> IA["Apply Interval Arithmetic Rule for Selected Operation"]
    AC2 --> IA
    IA --> OP1["Addition: Left plus Left, Right plus Right"]
    IA --> OP2["Subtraction: Left minus Right, Right minus Left"]
    IA --> OP3["Multiplication: Min of four products, Max of four products"]
    IA --> OP4["Division: Cross product Left by Right max, Right by Left min"]
    OP1 --> RES["Resultant Alpha Cut Interval"]
    OP2 --> RES
    OP3 --> RES
    OP4 --> RES
    RES --> OUT["Assemble Final Fuzzy Set Output"]

    style S1 fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,color:#000
    style S2 fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – Model Question]
**State and explain Zadeh's Extension Principle for a single variable mapping with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**
Zadeh's Extension Principle generalizes a crisp mapping $f: X \rightarrow Y$ to fuzzy sets. If $\tilde{A}$ is a fuzzy set in $X$ with membership $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$, then the image $\tilde{B} = f(\tilde{A})$ in $Y$ has the membership:

$$\mu_{\tilde{B}}(y) = \sup_{x \in f^{-1}(y)} \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \quad \text{if } f^{-1}(y) \neq \emptyset, \text{ else } 0$$

**Example:** Let $f(x) = x^2$ and $\tilde{A} = \text{"about 2"}$ on $\mathbb{R}^+$ given by $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = \max(0, 1 - |x - 2|)$. For $y = 4$, $f^{-1}(4) = \{-2, 2\}$. Only $x = 2$ lies in the support with $\mu = 1$, so $\mu_{\tilde{B}}(4) = 1$. For $y = 1$, $x = 1$, so $\mu_{\tilde{B}}(1) = 1 - |1 - 2| = 0$. For $y = 2.25$, $x = 1.5$, giving $\mu_{\tilde{B}}(2.25) = 0.5$. The result is the fuzzy set "about 4." **(3 marks)**

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Model Question]
**Define a fuzzy number. List and briefly explain the three defining properties.**

**Model Answer:**
A fuzzy number $\tilde{A}$ is a fuzzy subset of $\mathbb{R}$ satisfying:

1. **Normality:** There exists $x_0$ with $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x_0) = 1$ — the number is "achievable" with full certainty.
2. **Convexity:** $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda)x_2) \geq \min(\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x_1), \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x_2))$ — guarantees that the $\alpha$-cuts are intervals.
3. **Bounded Support & Continuity:** The set $\{x \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) > 0\}$ is bounded and $\mu_{\tilde{A}}$ is upper semi-continuous.

**Examples:** $\tilde{A} = (1, 3, 5)$ is a TFN; $\tilde{A} = (1, 2, 4, 5)$ is a TrFN. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Question]

**(a) [7 Marks]** State the multi-variable Extension Principle. Using it, derive the $\alpha$-cut of the sum of two fuzzy numbers $\tilde{A}$ and $\tilde{B}$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Given $\tilde{A} = (2, 4, 7)$ and $\tilde{B} = (1, 3, 6)$, compute the following using the $\alpha$-cut method and present the final fuzzy numbers:
(i) $\tilde{A} + \tilde{B}$
(ii) $\tilde{A} - \tilde{B}$

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Multi-Variable Extension Principle & Sum Derivation:**

**Statement:** For $f: X_1 \times X_2 \rightarrow Y$ and fuzzy sets $\tilde{A}_1, \tilde{A}_2$:

$$\mu_{\tilde{B}}(y) = \sup_{\substack{(x_1, x_2) \\ f(x_1, x_2) = y}} \min\left\{\mu_{\tilde{A}_1}(x_1), \mu_{\tilde{A}_2}(x_2)\right\}$$

**Derivation of Sum:** For the addition $f(x_1, x_2) = x_1 + x_2$, $f^{-1}(y) = \{(x_1, x_2) \mid x_1 + x_2 = y\}$. For a given $y$, we want the largest possible value of $\min(\mu_{\tilde{A}_1}(x_1), \mu_{\tilde{A}_2}(y - x_1))$. Let $x_1 \in A_\alpha$ and $y - x_1 \in B_\alpha$, i.e., $x_1 \in A_\alpha \cap (y - B_\alpha)$ where $y - B_\alpha = \{y - b \mid b \in B_\alpha\}$. This set is non-empty iff $A_\alpha \cap (y - B_\alpha) \neq \emptyset$, which is equivalent to:

$$y \in A_\alpha + B_\alpha = \{a + b \mid a \in A_\alpha, b \in B_\alpha\} = [a_L(\alpha) + b_L(\alpha), \ a_R(\alpha) + b_R(\alpha)]$$

So the $\alpha$-cut of the sum is the **Minkowski sum** of the $\alpha$-cuts:

$$(\tilde{A} + \tilde{B})_\alpha = [a_L(\alpha) + b_L(\alpha), \ a_R(\alpha) + b_R(\alpha)] \quad \square$$

**(b) Numerical Solutions:**

**(i) Addition:** $\alpha$-cuts:

$$A_\alpha = [2 + 2\alpha, \ 7 - 3\alpha]$$
$$B_\alpha = [1 + 2\alpha, \ 6 - 3\alpha]$$

$$(A + B)_\alpha = [2 + 2\alpha + 1 + 2\alpha, \ 7 - 3\alpha + 6 - 3\alpha] = [3 + 4\alpha, \ 13 - 6\alpha]$$

Matching the TFN template: $a = 3$, slope $4$ → $b = 7$, $c = 13$, slope $6$ → $b = 7$ ✓.

$$\boxed{\tilde{A} + \tilde{B} = (3, \ 7, \ 13)}$$

**(ii) Subtraction:** Using $(\tilde{A} - \tilde{B})_\alpha = [a_L - b_R, \ a_R - b_L]$:

$$(A - B)_\alpha = [(2 + 2\alpha) - (6 - 3\alpha), \ (7 - 3\alpha) - (1 + 2\alpha)]$$
$$(A - B)_\alpha = [2 + 2\alpha - 6 + 3\alpha, \ 7 - 3\alpha - 1 - 2\alpha] = [-4 + 5\alpha, \ 6 - 5\alpha]$$

Matching the template: $a = -4$, slope $5$ → $b = 1$, $c = 6$, slope $5$ → $b = 1$ ✓.

$$\boxed{\tilde{A} - \tilde{B} = (-4, \ 1, \ 6)}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Question]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the $\alpha$-cut representation of a fuzzy number. Derive the $\alpha$-cut expression for a trapezoidal fuzzy number $\tilde{A} = (a, b, c, d)$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Two triangular fuzzy numbers are $\tilde{A} = (1, 3, 5)$ and $\tilde{B} = (2, 4, 7)$. Compute $\tilde{A} \times \tilde{B}$ using the $\alpha$-cut method. State explicitly the type of fuzzy number obtained and write its membership function.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) $\alpha$-Cut of a TrFN:**

**Concept:** The $\alpha$-cut of any fuzzy number is the crisp interval of all points with membership $\geq \alpha$. For convex, normal fuzzy numbers, this interval is closed and bounded.

**Derivation for TrFN $\tilde{A} = (a, b, c, d)$, $a \leq b \leq c \leq d$:**

Membership function:

$$\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{x - a}{b - a}, & a \leq x \leq b \\ 1, & b \leq x \leq c \\ \dfrac{d - x}{d - c}, & c \leq x \leq d \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Left endpoint:** On $[a, b]$, $\dfrac{x - a}{b - a} \geq \alpha \Rightarrow x \geq a + \alpha(b - a)$.

**Right endpoint:** On $[c, d]$, $\dfrac{d - x}{d - c} \geq \alpha \Rightarrow x \leq d - \alpha(d - c)$.

$$\boxed{A_\alpha = [a + \alpha(b - a), \ d - \alpha(d - c)], \quad \alpha \in [0, 1]}$$

**(b) Multiplication of TFNs:**

$\alpha$-cuts:

$$A_\alpha = [1 + 2\alpha, \ 5 - 2\alpha]$$
$$B_\alpha = [2 + 2\alpha, \ 7 - 3\alpha]$$

Since all bounds are positive (for $\alpha \in [0,1]$), we use $(A \cdot B)_\alpha = [a_L b_L, a_R b_R]$:

$$(A \cdot B)_\alpha = [(1 + 2\alpha)(2 + 2\alpha), \ (5 - 2\alpha)(7 - 3\alpha)]$$

**Expanding:**

$$= [2 + 2\alpha + 4\alpha + 4\alpha^2, \ 35 - 15\alpha - 14\alpha + 6\alpha^2]$$
$$= [2 + 6\alpha + 4\alpha^2, \ 35 - 29\alpha + 6\alpha^2]$$

**Type of result:** Since the expressions contain $\alpha^2$ terms, the result is **NOT a triangular fuzzy number**. It is a **piecewise-quadratic (parabolic) fuzzy number**.

**Membership function:** For any $y$, we find $\alpha$ such that $y$ lies on the left or right curve.

- **Left curve** (from $y = 2$ to $y = 12$, the peak product $3 \times 4 = 12$): $y = 4\alpha^2 + 6\alpha + 2$
  - Solving for $\alpha$: $4\alpha^2 + 6\alpha + (2 - y) = 0$
  - $\alpha = \dfrac{-6 + \sqrt{36 - 16(2 - y)}}{8} = \dfrac{-6 + \sqrt{4 + 16y}}{8} = \dfrac{-3 + \sqrt{1 + 4y}}{4}$

- **Right curve** (from $y = 12$ to $y = 35$): $y = 6\alpha^2 - 29\alpha + 35$
  - Solving: $6\alpha^2 - 29\alpha + (35 - y) = 0$
  - $\alpha = \dfrac{29 - \sqrt{841 - 24(35 - y)}}{12} = \dfrac{29 - \sqrt{1 + 24y}}{12}$

Therefore the membership function is:

$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cdot \tilde{B}}(y) = \begin{cases} 0, & y < 2 \text{ or } y > 35 \\ \dfrac{\sqrt{1 + 4y} - 3}{4}, & 2 \leq y \leq 12 \\ \dfrac{29 - \sqrt{1 + 24y}}{12}, & 12 \leq y \leq 35 \end{cases}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Result: piecewise-quadratic (parabolic) fuzzy number, NOT a TFN.}}$$

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to flip the bounds in subtraction.** Many students write $(\tilde{A} - \tilde{B})_\alpha = [a_L - b_L, a_R - b_R]$ — this is **WRONG**. The correct formula is $[a_L - b_R, a_R - b_L]$. Loss: **2–3 marks** in Part B.
>
> 2. **Assuming product of TFNs is a TFN.** As we proved, the product of two positive TFNs is **quadratic**, not triangular. Stating "the result is a TFN" without checking loses **2 marks**.
>
> 3. **Mixing up interval multiplication's cross-product rule.** For positive intervals $[a,b] \cdot [c,d]$, the result is $[ac, bd]$, **NOT** $[ad, bc]$. Use all four products in the general (signed) case.
>
> 4. **Not stating the support condition for division.** Division requires $0 \notin B_\alpha$ for all $\alpha$. Always state this precondition.
>
> 5. **Confusing "min" with "min of four products".** In the general multiplication formula, the lower bound is the **minimum of the four products** $a_L b_L, a_L b_R, a_R b_L, a_R b_R$, not just the diagonal.
>
> 6. **Skipping the $\alpha$-cut derivation.** A 7-mark sub-part always expects the **explicit derivation** of the $\alpha$-cut, not just the final formula. Show the algebra of solving $\mu(x) \geq \alpha$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2**

- ⭐ **Extension Principle (Zadeh, 1975):** The bridge that lifts any crisp function $f$ to fuzzy sets. For multi-variable inputs, use $\min$ (t-norm) inside, $\sup$ (s-norm) outside.
- ⭐ **Fuzzy Number Definition:** Convex + Normal + Bounded Support + Upper Semi-Continuous → all $\alpha$-cuts are closed bounded intervals.
- ⭐ **$\alpha$-Cut of TFN $(a, b, c)$:** $\big[a + \alpha(b-a),\ c - \alpha(c-b)\big]$
- ⭐ **$\alpha$-Cut of TrFN $(a, b, c, d)$:** $\big[a + \alpha(b-a),\ d - \alpha(d-c)\big]$
- ⭐ **Addition rule:** $\big[a_L + b_L,\ a_R + b_R\big]$ — easy, symmetric, preserves TFN shape.
- ⭐ **Subtraction rule:** $\big[a_L - b_R,\ a_R - b_L\big]$ — **bounds flip**, easy to forget.
- ⭐ **Multiplication rule (positive):** $\big[a_L b_L,\ a_R b_R\big]$ — gives a **quadratic** result, not TFN.
- ⭐ **Multiplication rule (general):** Use $\min$ and $\max$ of all four corner products.
- ⭐ **Division rule (positive, $b_L > 0$):** $\big[\dfrac{a_L}{b_R},\ \dfrac{a_R}{b_L}\big]$ — **cross-flip**, result is hyperbolic.
- ⭐ **Scalar multiplication:** $\lambda > 0$ scales both ends; $\lambda < 0$ **swaps** the ends.
- ⭐ **Mnemonic for flips:** Subtraction and Division both **cross the diagonal** of the bound table.
- ⭐ **Result types:** $+ \to$ TFN, $- \to$ TFN, $\times \to$ Quadratic, $\div \to$ Hyperbolic, $\lambda \cdot \to$ TFN.
- ⭐ **Engineering Uses:** Fuzzy controllers, vague risk analysis, inventory with linguistic demand, imprecise measurement fusion, and financial forecasting.
- ⭐ **Examiner Traps:** Forgetting bound flips; wrongly assuming TFN closure under multiplication; skipping the $\alpha$-cut derivation steps.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuzzy Reasoning - Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP) and Generalized Modus Tollens (GMT)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT523 - FUZZY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fuzzy Reasoning - Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP) and Generalized Modus Tollens (GMT)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Big Picture: What is Fuzzy Reasoning?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fuzzy Reasoning** is the process of drawing conclusions ("inferring") from a set of fuzzy if-then rules and fuzzy facts, using fuzzy logic. It is the **inference engine** at the heart of every Fuzzy Inference System (FIS), such as the **Mamdani** and **Takagi–Sugeno–Kang (TSK)** models.

The two principal mechanisms of fuzzy reasoning that KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes are:
- **Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP)** — Forward / Direct Reasoning
- **Generalized Modus Tollens (GMT)** — Backward / Inverse Reasoning

Both are direct extensions of the classical syllogisms of Boolean logic.

---

## 1.2 Classical vs. Generalized: Bridging Crisp and Fuzzy Logic

### A. Classical (Crisp) Modus Ponens

$$
\frac{\text{Premise 1: } P \rightarrow Q \quad \text{Premise 2: } P}{\text{Conclusion: } Q}
$$

In plain words: *"If it is raining (P), then the road is wet (Q). It is raining (P). Therefore, the road is wet (Q)."* — exact, all-or-nothing matching.

### B. Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP)

$$
\frac{\text{Premise 1: If } x \text{ is } A \text{ then } y \text{ is } B \quad \text{Premise 2: } x \text{ is } A'}{\text{Conclusion: } y \text{ is } B'}
$$

Here, the input fact `A'` is **similar to but not identical to** the antecedent `A`, and the output `B'` is a **fuzzy, partial conclusion** derived from the rule.

### C. Classical (Crisp) Modus Tollens

$$
\frac{\text{Premise 1: } P \rightarrow Q \quad \text{Premise 2: } \lnot Q}{\text{Conclusion: } \lnot P}
$$

*"If it is raining, the road is wet. The road is NOT wet. Therefore, it is NOT raining."*

### D. Generalized Modus Tollens (GMT)

$$
\frac{\text{Premise 1: If } x \text{ is } A \text{ then } y \text{ is } B \quad \text{Premise 2: } y \text{ is } B'}{\text{Conclusion: } x \text{ is } A'}
$$

The observed output `B'` is **similar to** the consequent `B`, and we **infer backward** to estimate the likely input `A'`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (Real-World):**
> Imagine a **washing machine expert system** with the rule:
> *"If the cloth load is **HEAVY**, then the water level should be **HIGH**."*
> - **GMP**: You observe the load is *"somewhat heavy"* (not exactly HEAVY) → the system deduces the water level should be *"moderately high."*
> - **GMT**: You observe the water level is *"kind of low"* (not exactly HIGH) → the system deduces the load is probably *"not too heavy."*
> This is exactly how a fuzzy controller in a real LG or Whirlpool washing machine works!

---

## 1.3 Why "Generalized"?

The word **generalized** is critical. It captures two relaxations of the classical rules:

1. **Fuzzy sets replace crisp sets** — `A`, `B`, `A'`, `B'` are fuzzy sets with membership functions taking values in `[0, 1]`.
2. **The fact need not match the antecedent perfectly** — the input `A'` (or `B'`) is allowed to be **different from** the rule's `A` (or `B`). This is the essence of **approximate reasoning**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** GMP and GMT are categorized under **Fuzzy Inference / Approximate Reasoning** and form the theoretical backbone of the **Compositional Rule of Inference (CRI)** introduced by Zadeh (1973).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of the Reasoning Process

Both GMP and GMT operate on a **three-stage pipeline** inside the fuzzy inference engine:

1. **Fuzzification / Matching:** Compute the degree of similarity between the observed fact (`A'` or `B'`) and the rule's antecedent (`A`) or consequent (`B`).
2. **Implication Application:** Apply the fuzzy implication operator `I(A(x), B(y))` to combine the rule and the fact.
3. **Composition (Sup-Star / Sup-T-Norm):** Aggregate across all input universes using the **sup-star composition** to obtain the output fuzzy set (`B'` or `A'`).

---

## 2.2 The Compositional Rule of Inference (CRI) — Zadeh's Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The CRI is the **universal engine** that powers both GMP and GMT. Every fuzzy inference problem can be reduced to a sup-star composition.

The general form of the CRI is:

$$
R = A \rightarrow B
$$

$$
B' = A' \circ R
$$

where `∘` denotes the **sup-star composition**, defined as:

$$
B'(y) = \sup_{x \in X} \left[ A'(x) \star I(A(x), B(y)) \right]
$$

Here, `⋆` is a **t-norm** (most commonly minimum or algebraic product), and `I(·,·)` is a **fuzzy implication operator**.

---

## 2.3 The General Formulas for GMP and GMT

### A. Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP)

**Goal:** Given the rule `If x is A then y is B` and the observation `x is A'`, infer `y is B'`.

$$
B'(y) = \sup_{x \in X} \left[ A'(x) \star I(A(x), B(y)) \right]
$$

### B. Generalized Modus Tollens (GMT)

**Goal:** Given the rule `If x is A then y is B` and the observation `y is B'`, infer `x is A'`.

$$
A'(x) = \sup_{y \in Y} \left[ B'(y) \star I(A(x), B(y)) \right]
$$

---

## 2.4 Common Choice of Operators (KTU-Exam Favourite)

The implication operator and the t-norm are **design choices** that change the resulting output. The most frequent pairings are:

- **Mamdani Implication (Minimum-based):** $I(A(x), B(y)) = A(x) \wedge B(y) = \min(A(x), B(y))$
- **Larsen Implication (Product-based):** $I(A(x), B(y)) = A(x) \cdot B(y)$
- **Kleene-Dienes Implication (Material):** $I(a, b) = \max(1 - a, b)$
- **t-norm `⋆`:** minimum (`∧`) or algebraic product (`·`)

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Symbol / Concept** | **Mathematical Form** | **Where Used** |
|---|---|---|
| GMP — Output Membership | $B'(y) = \sup_{x} \left[ A'(x) \star I(A(x), B(y)) \right]$ | Forward inference |
| GMT — Output Membership | $A'(x) = \sup_{y} \left[ B'(y) \star I(A(x), B(y)) \right]$ | Backward inference |
| Mamdani Implication | $I(A(x), B(y)) = \min(A(x), B(y))$ | Most common in KTU problems |
| Larsen Implication | $I(A(x), B(y)) = A(x) \cdot B(y)$ | Product-based FIS |
| Min t-norm (⋆) | $a \star b = \min(a, b)$ | Mamdani-style FIS |
| Product t-norm (⋆) | $a \star b = a \cdot b$ | Larsen / product FIS |
| Sup-Star Composition | $B' = A' \circ (A \rightarrow B)$ | CRI engine |
| Classical MP Recovery | When $A' = A$, $B' = B$ (with min/product) | Sanity-check condition |
| Supremum operator | $\sup_{x \in X} f(x)$ — maximum over domain | Aggregation step |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Boundary Check (Sanity Condition):** When the observed fact exactly matches the rule premise — i.e., `A' = A` (for GMP) or `B' = B` (for GMT) — the generalized rule **must reduce to the classical rule** and yield `B' = B` (or `A' = A`). Use this to verify your calculations in the exam.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of GMP and GMT

| **Engineering Domain** | **Application** | **Reasoning Mode** |
|---|---|---|
| **Process Control** | Furnace temperature controllers, pH control | GMP (sensor → actuator) |
| **Automotive** | Anti-lock braking, traction control | GMP + GMT hybrid |
| **Consumer Electronics** | Washing machines, air conditioners, cameras | GMP (user setting → action) |
| **Medical Diagnosis** | Symptom → disease inference | GMP (forward) |
| **Fault Diagnosis** | Observation of output anomaly → root cause | **GMT (backward)** |
| **Decision Support** | Loan approval, risk analysis | Hybrid |
| **AI / Expert Systems** | Rule-based reasoning engines | Both |

> The key engineering insight: **GMP is the workhorse of fuzzy controllers** because real-world sensors are noisy and never give an exact match to the rule's antecedent — they give `A'`, and the controller must respond with `B'`.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: GMP with Mamdani Implication + Min t-norm

This is the **most asked configuration** in KTU examinations.

### Setup
- Universe: $X = \{x_1, x_2, x_3\}$, $Y = \{y_1, y_2, y_3\}$
- Antecedent: $A = [0.2, 0.7, 1.0]$
- Consequent: $B = [0.5, 0.9, 0.3]$
- Observed fact: $A' = [0.6, 0.4, 0.0]$
- Implication: Mamdani (min) → $I(A(x), B(y)) = \min(A(x), B(y))$
- t-norm: $\star = \min$

### Step 1: Compute the Implication Relation $R = A \rightarrow B$

The fuzzy relation $R(x, y)$ is an $m \times n$ matrix where each cell is $R(x_i, y_j) = \min(A(x_i), B(y_j))$.

$$
R(x_1, y_j) = \min(0.2, B(y_j)) = 0.2 \text{ for all } j
$$

$$
R(x_2, y_j) = \min(0.7, B(y_j)) = \min(0.7, [0.5, 0.9, 0.3]) = [0.5, 0.7, 0.3]
$$

$$
R(x_3, y_j) = \min(1.0, B(y_j)) = B = [0.5, 0.9, 0.3]
$$

Therefore, the full relation matrix is:

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 & 0.2 & 0.2 \\ 0.5 & 0.7 & 0.3 \\ 0.5 & 0.9 & 0.3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 2: Apply the Sup-Star Composition

The output is:

$$
B'(y_j) = \sup_{x_i} \left[ \min(A'(x_i), R(x_i, y_j)) \right]
$$

We compute column by column.

**For $y_1$ (column 1 of R = [0.2, 0.5, 0.5]):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Take } \min(A'(x_i), R(x_i, y_1)) \text{ for each } x_i: \\
x_1: & \quad \min(0.6, 0.2) = 0.2 \\
x_2: & \quad \min(0.4, 0.5) = 0.4 \\
x_3: & \quad \min(0.0, 0.5) = 0.0 \\
\text{Supremum:} \quad B'(y_1) = \max(0.2, 0.4, 0.0) = 0.4
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $y_2$ (column 2 of R = [0.2, 0.7, 0.9]):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1: & \quad \min(0.6, 0.2) = 0.2 \\
x_2: & \quad \min(0.4, 0.7) = 0.4 \\
x_3: & \quad \min(0.0, 0.9) = 0.0 \\
\text{Supremum:} \quad B'(y_2) = \max(0.2, 0.4, 0.0) = 0.4
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $y_3$ (column 3 of R = [0.2, 0.3, 0.3]):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1: & \quad \min(0.6, 0.2) = 0.2 \\
x_2: & \quad \min(0.4, 0.3) = 0.3 \\
x_3: & \quad \min(0.0, 0.3) = 0.0 \\
\text{Supremum:} \quad B'(y_3) = \max(0.2, 0.3, 0.0) = 0.3
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Final Result

$$
\boxed{B' = [0.4, 0.4, 0.3]}
$$

> **Sanity check:** Since `A' = [0.6, 0.4, 0.0]` is not equal to `A = [0.2, 0.7, 1.0]`, the output `B'` is **not** equal to `B`, confirming approximate (generalized) reasoning.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: GMT with Mamdani Implication + Min t-norm

### Setup
- Same $A$, $B$, $R$ as above
- Observed fact (output side): $B' = [0.0, 0.8, 0.2]$

### Step 1: Apply the GMT Formula

$$
A'(x_i) = \sup_{y_j} \left[ \min(B'(y_j), R(x_i, y_j)) \right]
$$

We compute row by row of R.

**For $x_1$ (row 1 of R = [0.2, 0.2, 0.2]):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_1: & \quad \min(0.0, 0.2) = 0.0 \\
y_2: & \quad \min(0.8, 0.2) = 0.2 \\
y_3: & \quad \min(0.2, 0.2) = 0.2 \\
\text{Supremum:} \quad A'(x_1) = \max(0.0, 0.2, 0.2) = 0.2
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $x_2$ (row 2 of R = [0.5, 0.7, 0.3]):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_1: & \quad \min(0.0, 0.5) = 0.0 \\
y_2: & \quad \min(0.8, 0.7) = 0.7 \\
y_3: & \quad \min(0.2, 0.3) = 0.2 \\
\text{Supremum:} \quad A'(x_2) = \max(0.0, 0.7, 0.2) = 0.7
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $x_3$ (row 3 of R = [0.5, 0.9, 0.3]):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_1: & \quad \min(0.0, 0.5) = 0.0 \\
y_2: & \quad \min(0.8, 0.9) = 0.8 \\
y_3: & \quad \min(0.2, 0.3) = 0.2 \\
\text{Supremum:} \quad A'(x_3) = \max(0.0, 0.8, 0.2) = 0.8
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Final Result

$$
\boxed{A' = [0.2, 0.7, 0.8]}
$$

> **Sanity check:** Since `B' = [0.0, 0.8, 0.2]` is not equal to `B = [0.5, 0.9, 0.3]`, the inferred input `A'` is **not** equal to the original `A`, again confirming approximate (backward) reasoning.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: GMP vs. GMT — Side-by-Side Comparison

| **Aspect** | **GMP** | **GMT** |
|---|---|---|
| Direction of Reasoning | Forward (Antecedent → Consequent) | Backward (Consequent → Antecedent) |
| Input | $A'$ (similar to $A$) | $B'$ (similar to $B$) |
| Output | $B'$ | $A'$ |
| Core Formula | $B'(y) = \sup_x [A'(x) \star I(A(x), B(y))]$ | $A'(x) = \sup_y [B'(y) \star I(A(x), B(y))]$ |
| Operation on Relation $R$ | **Column-wise** supremum | **Row-wise** supremum |
| Equivalent Linear Algebra | $B' = A' \cdot R$ (row vector × matrix) | $A' = R \cdot B'$ (matrix × column vector) |
| Primary Application | **Fuzzy Control** (sensors → actuators) | **Diagnosis / Abductive Reasoning** |
| Classical Reduction | $A' = A \Rightarrow B' = B$ | $B' = B \Rightarrow A' = A$ |
| Difficulty Level (KTU) | Moderate | Moderate to High |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flow Diagram: Fuzzy Reasoning Pipeline (GMP)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Fuzzy Rule Base: If x is A then y is B"] --> B["Fuzzy Implication Operator I(A, B)"]
    A2["Observed Fact: x is A-prime"] --> C["Matching with Antecedent A"]
    C --> D["Apply t-norm with Relation R"]
    B --> D
    D --> E["Sup-Star Composition: supremum over x"]
    E --> F["Output Fuzzy Set: y is B-prime"]
    F --> G["Defuzzification (if needed)"]
    G --> H["Crisp Output Value"]
```

## 4.2 Flow Diagram: Fuzzy Reasoning Pipeline (GMT)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Fuzzy Rule Base: If x is A then y is B"] --> B["Fuzzy Implication Operator I(A, B)"]
    A2["Observed Output: y is B-prime"] --> C["Matching with Consequent B"]
    C --> D["Apply t-norm with Relation R"]
    B --> D
    D --> E["Sup-Star Composition: supremum over y"]
    E --> F["Inferred Fuzzy Set: x is A-prime"]
    F --> G["Defuzzification (if needed)"]
    G --> H["Estimated Input Cause"]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: CRI Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT SIDE"]
        A1["Linguistic Variable x"] --> A2["Fuzzification"]
        A2 --> A3["Fuzzy Set A-prime"]
    end
    subgraph CORE["INFERENCE ENGINE"]
        B1["Rule Base"] --> B2["Implication: I(A, B)"]
        B2 --> B3["Fuzzy Relation R"]
        A3 --> B4["Compositional Rule of Inference"]
        B3 --> B4
        B4 --> B5["Sup-Star Operator"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT SIDE"]
        B5 --> C1["Fuzzy Set B-prime"]
        C1 --> C2["Defuzzification"]
        C2 --> C3["Crisp Output y"]
    end
```

## 4.4 Comparison Tree: GMP vs GMT Reasoning Modes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R["Fuzzy Reasoning"] --> R1["GMP: Forward"]
    R --> R2["GMT: Backward"]
    R1 --> R1A["Input: A-prime, similar to A"]
    R1 --> R1B["Output: B-prime, inferred consequent"]
    R1 --> R1C["Used in: Fuzzy Controllers"]
    R2 --> R2A["Input: B-prime, similar to B"]
    R2 --> R2B["Output: A-prime, inferred antecedent"]
    R2 --> R2C["Used in: Fault Diagnosis"]
    R1A --> S1["A-prime o R using sup-min"]
    R2A --> S2["R o B-prime using sup-min"]
    S1 --> OUT["Result: Generalized Inference"]
    S2 --> OUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> These block-level schematics depict the **functional architecture** of the CRI pipeline. They map the data-flow interactions between fuzzification, implication, composition, and defuzzification in a Mamdani-style fuzzy inference system.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between classical Modus Ponens and Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP) with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Classical Modus Ponens** | **Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP)** |
|---|---|---|
| Rule form | If P then Q (crisp) | If x is A then y is B (fuzzy) |
| Fact | P (exactly) | x is A' (similar to A) |
| Conclusion | Q (exactly) | y is B' (fuzzy output) |
| Logic | Boolean (0/1) | Fuzzy (membership in [0,1]) |

**Example:**
- **Classical:** If temperature > 100°C, then water boils. Temperature = 105°C. → Water boils.
- **GMP:** If temperature is *HIGH* then water is *BOILING*. Temperature is *moderately high*. → Water is *somewhat boiling*.

(1 mark each for crisp definition, fuzzy definition, and example)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**State the mathematical formulation of Generalized Modus Tollens (GMT) and explain the role of the sup-star composition.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Mathematical formulation (2 marks):**

$$
A'(x) = \sup_{y \in Y} \left[ B'(y) \star I(A(x), B(y)) \right]
$$

where:
- $A, B$ are fuzzy sets in the rule "If x is A then y is B"
- $B'$ is the observed fact on the consequent side
- $A'$ is the inferred fuzzy set on the antecedent side
- $I(\cdot, \cdot)$ is the fuzzy implication operator
- $\star$ is a t-norm

**Role of sup-star composition (1 mark):** The sup-star composition aggregates the matching degree between $B'$ and the consequent $B(y)$ for every possible input $x$, selecting the **best-supported** value of $A'(x)$ across the entire output universe $Y$. The supremum captures the strongest inference possible.

---

## 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP)** with its mathematical formulation. Discuss how it generalizes the classical Modus Ponens and outline its significance in fuzzy control systems. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the following fuzzy sets and rule, compute the output of GMP using the **Mamdani implication** and **min t-norm** with the sup-star composition. **(7 marks)**

- Rule: *If x is A then y is B*
- $A = \{1/1 + 0.6/2 + 0.2/3\}$
- $B = \{0.3/1 + 0.7/2 + 1.0/3\}$
- Observation: $A' = \{0.5/1 + 0.8/2 + 0.3/3\}$

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Theory (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP) is the fuzzy extension of classical Modus Ponens and is the foundation of forward fuzzy reasoning.

$$
\frac{\text{Premise 1: If } x \text{ is } A \text{ then } y \text{ is } B \quad \text{Premise 2: } x \text{ is } A'}{\text{Conclusion: } y \text{ is } B'}
$$

**Mathematical Formulation (2 marks):**

$$
B'(y) = \sup_{x \in X} \left[ A'(x) \star I(A(x), B(y)) \right]
$$

**Generalization (1.5 marks):** GMP relaxes the requirement of exact matching — the observed fact $A'$ need not equal $A$. The output $B'$ is a fuzzy set obtained by combining the rule and the fact through the sup-star composition. When $A' = A$, GMP reduces exactly to classical MP.

**Significance in Fuzzy Control (1.5 marks):** Real-world sensor inputs are noisy and rarely match the rule's antecedent exactly. GMP enables a fuzzy controller (e.g., a temperature controller) to **produce a smooth, graded response** $B'$ from a partial or imprecise observation $A'$, which is essential for stability and avoiding abrupt switching in control actions.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark): Compute the Implication Relation R using Mamdani (min) implication.**

$R(x_i, y_j) = \min(A(x_i), B(y_j))$

- $R(x_1, y_*) = \min(1.0, B) = [0.3, 0.7, 1.0]$
- $R(x_2, y_*) = \min(0.6, B) = [0.3, 0.6, 0.6]$
- $R(x_3, y_*) = \min(0.2, B) = [0.2, 0.2, 0.2]$

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3 & 0.7 & 1.0 \\ 0.3 & 0.6 & 0.6 \\ 0.2 & 0.2 & 0.2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** State the sup-min composition formula.

$$
B'(y_j) = \max_{x_i} \left[ \min(A'(x_i), R(x_i, y_j)) \right]
$$

**Step 3 (4 marks): Compute column by column.**

**Column $y_1$ (R = [0.3, 0.3, 0.2]):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1: & \quad \min(0.5, 0.3) = 0.3 \\
x_2: & \quad \min(0.8, 0.3) = 0.3 \\
x_3: & \quad \min(0.3, 0.2) = 0.2 \\
B'(y_1) = & \max(0.3, 0.3, 0.2) = 0.3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Column $y_2$ (R = [0.7, 0.6, 0.2]):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1: & \quad \min(0.5, 0.7) = 0.5 \\
x_2: & \quad \min(0.8, 0.6) = 0.6 \\
x_3: & \quad \min(0.3, 0.2) = 0.2 \\
B'(y_2) = & \max(0.5, 0.6, 0.2) = 0.6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Column $y_3$ (R = [1.0, 0.6, 0.2]):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1: & \quad \min(0.5, 1.0) = 0.5 \\
x_2: & \quad \min(0.8, 0.6) = 0.6 \\
x_3: & \quad \min(0.3, 0.2) = 0.2 \\
B'(y_3) = & \max(0.5, 0.6, 0.2) = 0.6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark): Final Answer**

$$
\boxed{B' = \{0.3/1 + 0.6/2 + 0.6/3\}}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**
`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PECMT523]`

**(a)** Explain the **Generalized Modus Tollens (GMT)** with its mathematical formulation. Describe how the sup-star composition works row-wise (in contrast to GMP) and discuss a real-world application where GMT is preferred over GMP. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the rule *"If x is A then y is B"* and the following fuzzy sets. Using the **Mamdani implication** and **min t-norm**, compute the result of **GMT** given the observed fact $B' = \{0.4/1 + 0.9/2 + 0.2/3\}$. **(7 marks)**

- $A = \{0.2/1 + 0.7/2 + 1.0/3\}$
- $B = \{0.5/1 + 0.9/2 + 0.3/3\}$

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Theory (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Generalized Modus Tollens (GMT) performs **backward / abductive** reasoning. Given the rule and a partial observation of the consequent $B'$, it infers a fuzzy description $A'$ of the antecedent.

$$
\frac{\text{Premise 1: If } x \text{ is } A \text{ then } y \text{ is } B \quad \text{Premise 2: } y \text{ is } B'}{\text{Conclusion: } x \text{ is } A'}
$$

**Mathematical Formulation (2 marks):**

$$
A'(x) = \sup_{y \in Y} \left[ B'(y) \star I(A(x), B(y)) \right]
$$

**Row-wise Sup-Star (1.5 marks):** In GMT, the composition evaluates $B'(y)$ against each **row** of the relation matrix $R(x, y)$ for a fixed $x$, then takes the supremum over $y$.
